_meta.title: Virtual Memory — TLB, Page Tables, MMU

questions.question: A 32-bit system uses 4 KB pages. How many bits are used for the page offset?
questions.explanation: 4 KB = 2^12, so the offset needs 12 bits. The remaining 20 bits form the virtual page number.

questions.question: In a TLB with 4-way set associativity and 64 total entries, how many sets are there?
questions.explanation: Sets = total entries / associativity = 64 / 4 = 16 sets. Each set holds 4 entries (ways).

questions.question: A 2-level page table is used instead of a single-level table primarily because:
options: It is always faster to look up
It wastes less memory on unused (sparse) regions of the address space
It eliminates the need for a TLB
It reduces the page offset size
questions.explanation: A single-level 32-bit table with 4 KB pages needs 4 MB always. A 2-level table only allocates page tables for directory entries that are actually used, saving memory for sparse address spaces.

questions.question: When a page fault occurs, what is the approximate cost compared to a TLB hit?
questions.explanation: A TLB hit takes ~1 cycle. A page fault requires a disk access (~10 ms ≈ 10,000,000 cycles at 1 GHz). This is roughly 10 million times slower, which is why avoiding page faults is critical.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)?
options: To store frequently used data values
To cache recent virtual-to-physical address translations, avoiding slow page table walks
To buffer I/O operations to disk
questions.explanation: The TLB is a small, fast cache that stores recent page table entries. Every memory access requires a virtual-to-physical translation, and a full page table walk (4 levels on x86-64) is expensive. The TLB typically hits in 1 cycle, making this translation nearly free.
